skill.name: cli-design-expert
skill.description: Expert guidance for designing command-line and terminal interfaces with clear command structures, help text, errors, prompts, output formats, and release checklists.
skill.summary: Design professional CLI and TUI experiences with clear commands, help, errors, prompts, and output.
skill.author: DammianMiller

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, TypeScript, command-substitution, reconnaissance, weak-crypto, and URL patterns in SKILL.md. Manual review found these are instructional Markdown examples, checklist text, shell-completion templates, and documentation links, with no executable scripts, hidden network behavior, credential access, or prompt-injection attempt. The skill has low residual risk because it teaches users to implement CLIs that may execute commands in their own projects.

low_findings.title: Static Command Patterns Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external-command detections occur inside fenced Bash or TypeScript examples that show CLI syntax, exit codes, help output, prompts, and shell completion. They are not installed scripts and do not run automatically when the skill is used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched content is visibly contained in Markdown code fences and is presented as guidance examples. No separate executable file or auto-run instruction was found.

low_findings.title: Network URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The two hardcoded URLs are GitHub documentation links inside an error-message example. They do not send data, invoke a network client, or instruct hidden exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URL hits are string values assigned to a docs field in a displayed TypeScript example. No fetch, curl, webhook, or credential transmission behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported weak-cryptographic and reconnaissance indicators, but manual review found headings, CLI examples, checklist terms, and ordinary TypeScript examples. No cryptographic implementation, host probing, network scanning, or secret collection was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations do not contain crypto routines or reconnaissance workflows. The hits appear to be keyword matches against benign prose and examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Example Shell Completion Uses Command Substitution
dangerous_patterns.description: A shell-completion template includes command substitution for compgen output. This is a common Bash completion pattern and is low risk here because it is embedded as instructional example text, not executed by the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is a real command-substitution pattern, but it is escaped inside a TypeScript template example. Human review should ensure users adapt it safely in generated projects.

content.value_statement: Poor command-line interfaces slow users down and make errors hard to recover from. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design clear CLI and TUI workflows with practical UX patterns.
actual_capabilities: Recommends command and subcommand structures for developer tools.
Provides patterns for flags, options, exit codes, and shell completion.
Shows how to write layered help text with examples and common fixes.
Guides error messages that explain the problem and next action.
Covers interactive prompts, confirmations, tables, colors, and JSON output.
Supplies a release checklist for CLI quality review.
limitations: It provides design guidance, not a complete CLI framework or runtime.
Examples use TypeScript and common Node.js libraries, so other stacks need adaptation.
It does not test generated commands or validate security behavior automatically.
It does not include platform-specific accessibility testing for every terminal.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Developer CLI
use_cases.description: Define command groups, flags, help text, output modes, and error behavior before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Improve an Existing Terminal Workflow
use_cases.description: Review confusing commands and redesign them with clearer names, examples, validation, and recovery paths.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team CLI Guidelines
use_cases.description: Create repeatable rules for command naming, colors, JSON output, confirmations, and release checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Simple Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this CLI command for usability. Suggest clearer naming, flags, help text, and error messages: [paste command design].

prompt_templates.title: Design Command Structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate planning for a new tool.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a command structure for a CLI that helps users [describe task]. Include subcommands, arguments, flags, examples, and exit codes.

prompt_templates.title: Rewrite Help and Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite the help output and error messages for this CLI. Make the output example-driven, concise, and useful for recovery: [paste current output].

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Full CLI Experience
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this CLI specification for professional UX. Cover command hierarchy, prompt flow, destructive actions, output formats, colors, accessibility, and scripting support: [paste spec].

output_examples.input: A request to design commands for a project initialization tool.
output: A command hierarchy with root help, init options, and focused subcommands.
Recommended short and long flags for common actions.
Example help text that includes common workflows and recovery guidance.

output_examples.input: A confusing error message from an existing CLI.
output: A rewritten error with the failing condition, searched location, and next command to run.
A suggestion for the correct exit code and documentation link placement.
A note on avoiding noisy stack traces during normal usage errors.

output_examples.input: A release checklist request for a terminal tool.
output: A checklist covering help, exit codes, confirmations, JSON output, quiet mode, colors, progress, and validation.
Specific acceptance criteria for each CLI quality area.

best_practices: Start with the main user workflows, then map commands and flags to those workflows.
Provide examples in every detailed help page and show recovery steps for common failures.
Support both human-readable output and stable JSON output for scripts.
anti_patterns: Do not hide important behavior behind unclear flags or overloaded commands.
Do not perform destructive actions without confirmation or an explicit force option.
Do not use colors, spinners, or tables in ways that break quiet or machine-readable output.

faq.question: What kind of CLI work is this skill best for?
faq.answer: It is best for command design, help text, error messages, interactive prompts, output formatting, and release review.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete working CLI?
faq.answer: No. It gives implementation patterns and design guidance that a developer can adapt to a chosen framework.

faq.question: Can it help with terminal user interfaces?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide terminal interaction patterns, prompts, progress indicators, semantic colors, and layout decisions.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. The skill text includes documentation links, but no runtime network operation is required.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Review generated command examples before implementation, especially shell completion, file operations, and destructive actions.
